skill.name: git-commit-helper
skill.description: Helps write clear Conventional Commit messages by reviewing staged Git changes and applying commit message best practices.
skill.summary: Create concise, structured Git commit messages from staged changes.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found these are Markdown examples, commit templates, or ordinary Git commands for the stated purpose. No prompt injection, network access, credential access, or malicious intent was found. The remaining risk is low because some documented Git commands can alter repository history or create commits when run by the user.

low_findings.title: Legitimate Git Commands Can Modify Repository State
low_findings.description: The skill documents Git commands such as git add -p, git commit, and git commit --amend. These are appropriate for a commit helper, but they can create commits, stage changes, or rewrite the latest commit if a user runs them without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly visible and can mutate repository state. The context is legitimate Git workflow guidance, so this is a low risk rather than a malicious pattern.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography detections correspond to normal prose, commit format placeholders, or example commit message text. No hashing, encryption, crypto library usage, or security-sensitive algorithm selection was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic operation at the reported locations. The matches appear to come from generic words or placeholders, not crypto code.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Matches Are Mostly Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: The reported backtick execution locations are fenced Markdown code blocks or inline code examples. They present Git commands and commit message examples rather than executable Ruby or shell code embedded in a script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation and the flagged backticks delimit examples. No Ruby code, command substitution, or automatic execution path was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository-Mutating Git Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes user-run examples for git add -p, git commit, and git commit --amend. These commands are expected for this skill but should be used only after reviewing staged changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present in clear Git workflow examples. Their effect is well understood, but the skill does not run them automatically.

content.value_statement: Poor commit messages make code history hard to understand. This skill reviews staged changes and guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code toward clear Conventional Commit messages.
actual_capabilities: Reviews staged Git diffs before suggesting a commit message.
Applies Conventional Commit types such as feat, fix, docs, refactor, test, and chore.
Suggests scopes based on the affected area of the codebase.
Adds optional body text that explains the reason and impact of a change.
Handles examples for breaking changes, multi-file commits, and selective staging.
limitations: It depends on the staged diff being available and relevant.
It does not verify that tests passed or that code behavior is correct.
It cannot infer private issue context unless the user provides it.
It may need user review for large or unrelated change sets.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Feature Commit
use_cases.description: Turn a staged feature diff into a clear Conventional Commit with a concise summary and useful body.

use_cases.title: Review a Bug Fix Before Commit
use_cases.description: Summarize a staged fix and choose a focused scope that matches the affected component.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Commit History
use_cases.description: Use the same format and checklist across documentation, tests, refactors, and infrastructure changes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Commit Message
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one message from staged changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staged changes and suggest one Conventional Commit message with a short subject line.

prompt_templates.title: Message With Body
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request that needs context and impact.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the staged diff and write a Conventional Commit message with a subject and body. Explain why the change was made.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Type and Scope
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for type and scope reasoning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staged changes, choose the best commit type and scope, and explain any alternatives you rejected.

prompt_templates.title: Split Commit Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for mixed or multi-file changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the staged diff and tell me whether it should be split into multiple commits. Suggest commit messages for each logical change.

output_examples.input: Staged changes add login validation and token refresh tests.
output_examples.output: Suggested message: feat(auth): add login validation tests. Body: Cover token refresh behavior and invalid credential handling.

output_examples.input: Staged changes fix a null profile crash in the API response handler.
output_examples.output: Suggested message: fix(api): handle empty user profiles. Body: Prevent profile rendering failures when optional fields are missing.

output_examples.input: Staged changes update CI configuration and Node version.
output_examples.output: Suggested message: chore(ci): update Node runtime. Body: Align the pipeline with the supported runtime version.

best_practices: Review git diff --staged before asking for a final commit message.
Keep each commit focused on one logical change whenever possible.
Include a body when the reason or migration impact is not obvious.
anti_patterns: Do not commit unrelated formatting, feature, and fix changes together.
Do not accept vague subjects such as update, changes, or fixes.
Do not amend commits on shared branches without understanding team workflow.

faq.question: Does this skill create commits automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. The user decides whether to run Git commands.

faq.question: Does it support Conventional Commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses type, optional scope, short description, body, and breaking change guidance.

faq.question: Can it help split a large commit?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review staged changes and suggest logical commit boundaries.

faq.question: Does it read unstaged changes?
faq.answer: The workflow focuses on staged changes. Ask separately if you want unstaged changes reviewed.

faq.question: Can it include issue numbers?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the issue number or ticket ID, and it can include it in the message.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: It does not include network behavior. Review generated messages before sharing sensitive project details.
